1: The superscription of Psalm 142 is one of the most historically specific in the Psalter: "A Maskil of David, a prayer composed when he was in the cave." Two cave episodes are associated with David in 1 Samuel: the Cave of Adullam (1 Samuel 22:1–2), where David fled from Saul and was joined by 400 men, and the cave at En-gedi (1 Samuel 24:3–22), where David had the opportunity to kill Saul but refrained. Both involve hiding from mortal danger with no human rescue in sight. "With full voice (qôlî)" — the emphasis on audible crying to YHWH, even in hiding, when quiet would be safer, suggests that this prayer is the desperate act of one who has abandoned all pretense of strategy and fallen back on YHWH alone.
2: "I pour out my troubles before him; I lay my distress before him" — ʾešpōk lĕpānāyw śîḥî (I pour out my complaint before him). The "pouring out" idiom for prayer appears in 1 Samuel 1:15, where Hannah says "I have been pouring out my soul before the LORD" (šōpeket et-napšî), and in Lamentations 2:19 ("pour out your heart like water before the face of the LORD"). The visceral image of liquid poured out suggests total self-disclosure — nothing held back, the entire content of the inner life offered to the divine listener. Śîaḥ (complaint, meditation, prayer) is related to the verb for "speaking" or "rehearsing" — the prayer is the spoken articulation of what had been roiling silently within.
3: "When my spirit grows faint within me, you know my way" — bĕhitʿaṭṭēp ʿālay rûḥî wĕʾattāh yādaʿtā nĕtîbātî. "Spirit grows faint (hitʿaṭṭēp)" — the word means to wrap oneself, to be covered over, faint; it appears also in Psalms 61:2 ("when my heart is faint") and Psalms 77:3 ("my spirit grows faint"). The confession "you know my way (yādaʿtā nĕtîbātî)" — the hidden path in the cave is known to YHWH even when the speaker cannot see it. Divine omniscience as the ground of prayer: the one who knows my hidden path knows the trap hidden in it (Psalms 139:1–4).
4: "I look to my right — no one acknowledges me; all escape is cut off from me; not a soul cares for me" — the three-fold description of total abandonment: no acknowledgment (ûmakîr, no one recognizes me), no escape (ʾābad mānôs mimmennî, escape has perished from me), and no one who cares for my soul (ʾên dôrēš lĕnapšî, no one seeks after my soul). In ANE culture, the "right side" was the position of the advocate, the helper, the witness who would stand up for you in legal proceedings (Psalms 109:31; Psalms 110:5). To have no one at your right is to have no one to defend you — a legal, social, and existential abandonment.
5: "I cry to you, O LORD; I say, 'You are my shelter and my portion in this living world'" — ḥelqî beʾereṣ haḥayyîm (my portion in the land of the living). Ḥēleq (portion, share, lot) is the technical term for the tribal allotment of land in the conquest and settlement of Canaan (Joshua 14:4; Psalms 15:13; Psalms 18:9); it is also used for the Levites, who have "no portion" of land because YHWH is their portion (Numbers 18:20; Deuteronomy 10:9; Psalms 18:1–2; Psalms 16:5). To declare "you are my portion" is to claim the Levitical inheritance — having YHWH rather than land as the ground of existence. "Land of the living (ʾereṣ haḥayyîm)" — the counterpart to Sheol and the "land of forgetting" (Psalms 88:12); the sphere of covenant life before YHWH.
6: "Hear my cry, for I have been brought very low; rescue me from those who pursue me, for they are too strong for me" — kî dallôtî mĕʾōd (for I have been brought very low, am very weak). Dal (weak, poor, lowly) is the social category of those without resources or power — not the moderate poverty of the ʿānî but extreme helplessness. "Too strong for me (kî ʾāmĕṣû mimmennî)" — the honest admission that the enemies are stronger than the speaker is the ground for the prayer: when human resources are exhausted, the appeal to divine power becomes the only option. The "prayer of the weak" is structurally central to the Psalter (Psalms 34:6; Psalms 40:17; Psalms 70:5; Psalms 86:1; Psalms 109:22).
7: "Lead my soul out of this prison so I can praise your name. Then the righteous will gather around me, because you will have been generous to me" — hôṣîʾāh mimmāsger napšî (lead out my soul from the prison/enclosure). The cave has become a prison — the hiding place that was refuge is also confinement. The liberation sought is not merely physical escape but the restoration of the capacity for praise (lĕhôdôt ʾet-šĕmekā) and communal worship (the righteous gathering around the delivered person). The psalm ends with a vision of restored community: the one who prayed in utter isolation (Psalms 142:4: "not a soul cares for me") will be surrounded by the righteous. Liberation from the cave enables the gathering of a congregation.
